coredata.dc:title: Efficient high-fidelity modeling of a nickel-rich silicon-graphite cell enabled by optimal spatial distribution

coredata.dc:description:
The development of high-fidelity battery models with a unique set of parameters remains challenging, especially when considering operation at various C-rates as well as relaxation periods, which becomes more complex in the case of cells with silicon-graphite blended anodes. In this paper, we present a general reduced-order modeling framework that allows including blended electrodes, a particle size distribution, and self-heating, based on a discrete transmission line model. The optimal number of spatial nodes in each electrode is determined to be 6 for both electrodes, thereby challenging the volume averaging hypothesis. A particle size distribution is required to match the slower relaxation response observed experimentally; however, self-heating and spatial distribution are also shown to contribute to a faster effective relaxation at higher C-rates. The proposed model provides highly accurate results when validated against experimental data: below 15 mV Root Mean Square (RMS) for discharges from C/2 up to 2C followed by relaxation, below 13 mV RMS for charges at C/2 and 1C, and below 0.35
∘
C RMS for cell temperature in all cases. Finally, the potential risk of lithium plating is discussed, suggesting that the presence of silicon may lead to an earlier onset thereof. In summary, the importance of an appropriate description of spatial distribution is demonstrated for an accurate modeling of a commercial Nickel Manganese Cobalt/Si-Gr (NMC/Si-Gr) cell.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The development of high-fidelity battery models with a unique set of parameters remains challenging, especially when considering operation at various C-rates as well as relaxation periods, which becomes more complex in the case of cells with silicon-graphite blended anodes. In this paper, we present a general reduced-order modeling framework that allows including blended electrodes, a particle size distribution, and self-heating, based on a discrete transmission line model. The optimal number of spatial nodes in each electrode is determined to be 6 for both electrodes, thereby challenging the volume averaging hypothesis. A particle size distribution is required to match the slower relaxation response observed experimentally; however, self-heating and spatial distribution are also shown to contribute to a faster effective relaxation at higher C-rates. The proposed model provides highly accurate results when validated against experimental data: below 15 mV Root Mean Square (RMS) for discharges from C/2 up to 2C followed by relaxation, below 13 mV RMS for charges at C/2 and 1C, and below 0.35
∘
C RMS for cell temperature in all cases. Finally, the potential risk of lithium plating is discussed, suggesting that the presence of silicon may lead to an earlier onset thereof. In summary, the importance of an appropriate description of spatial distribution is demonstrated for an accurate modeling of a commercial Nickel Manganese Cobalt/Si-Gr (NMC/Si-Gr) cell.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Silicon constitutes a promising active material for high-energy battery anodes given its specific capacity (3579 mAh
⋅
g-1), tenfold that of conventional graphite [1], as well as its low operating voltage against Li/Li
+
(0.4 V) [2]. Nevertheless, silicon particles undergo significant volume fluctuations ( ≥ 300 %) after lithiation and delithiation [3], accelerating the loss of active material due to particle cracking and structural instability of the solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) [4]. Despite ongoing research efforts to create silicon-rich anodes [5,6], a current approach to enhance anode energy density consists of combining conventional graphite with small mass fractions of silicon, in proportions ranging from 0 wt% to 20 wt% [7], thus benefiting from the significantly lower volume expansion of graphite during lithiation (approximately 13 %) [8], and increasing electrode mechanical stability [9]. Therefore, silicon-graphite (Si-Gr) blended anodes are now incorporated into commercial cells, achieving energy densities close to 250 Wh/kg when combined with nickel-rich cathodes such as NMC811 [10,11]. Nevertheless, the mechanisms of silicon (de)lithiation and hysteresis, along with the intricate interactions between silicon and graphite during cell operation, remain an open research topic [12]. Hence, the development of accurate physics-based models is essential for a more comprehensive insight into the behavior of composite electrodes, including the current distribution between both materials, as well as the degree of utilization of each in a wide range of operating conditions.

A first approach to the modeling of blended electrodes was presented in [13], where the original Doyle–Fuller–Newman (DFN) model [14,15] was adapted to include separate instances of solid diffusion and charge transfer for each material in the positive electrode. Jung [16] expanded this approach by proposing an equilibrium model to determine quasi-static characteristics for different blend ratios, and the dynamic behavior was simulated numerically within the DFN framework. Reduced-order models for blended electrodes have also been developed by extending the single-particle model (SPM) to several particles [17,18], or by extending physics-based transfer functions for standard electrodes [19], with different approximations for the computation of the current split between both materials. Furthermore, an empirical equivalent circuit model (ECM) framework for blended electrodes was presented in [20] by defining one parallel branch for each material, each consisting of an Open Circuit Potential (OCP) source in series with parallel-RC elements to model dynamic behavior. However, these previous works were mostly concerned with the modeling of a composite positive electrode, and did not incorporate specific features of the blended Si-Gr anode. The DFN modeling framework with separate functions for lithium diffusion and charge transfer for each phase was applied to develop a composite electrode model for cells with a blended Si-Gr in [21], providing information on the complex interactions between silicon and graphite and the current split at different State of Charge (SOC) ranges. This approach has also been the starting point of more complex models incorporating thermal and mechanical degradation mechanisms [22–24]. Nevertheless, the reproduction of the experimental voltage response at different C-rates with a unique set of parameters still remains challenging, especially at low SOCs, which is apparent in model validation results for discharges [21,23] and charges [22,24], as well as relaxation profiles [25]. This indicates that there exists room for improvement in the physical description and predictive ability of previous approaches, since an appropriate quantification of silicon utilization may be critical in the calculation of loss of active material owing to volume fluctuations due to lithiation and delithiation, as well as the fast charging limitations because of lithium plating [26].

As stated above, the most widely accepted alternatives for physics-based modeling are the DFN and the SPM, with the main difference between them being the consideration of a spatial distribution of active material particles within the electrode thickness. Hence, the solid diffusion process may have different boundary conditions for interfacial current density at different locations in the DFN model, which may lead to sequential lithiation/delithiation; whereas in the SPM all particles are subject to the same boundary conditions, leading to simultaneous operation that allows accounting for just one representative particle [27,28]. The extended SPM (SPMe) is usually considered accurate in relation to the DFN model for currents up to 2C [27,29]; however, complex phenomena are observed experimentally at moderate to high current rates, such as an apparent smoothing of the Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) [30], as well as an increase in the slope of the voltage profile vs. time for materials with a largely flat OCP such as lithium-iron-phosphate [31] or stepwise flat such as graphite [32], due to sequential operation ascribed to low matrix conductivity. This behavior cannot be replicated by an SPM-type model, which indicates that an appropriate spatial distribution is essential for the development of models with a wide range of applicability. In this regard, a discrete spatial distribution is consistent with results from Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests as shown in [30], by interpreting the observed ohmic resistance (i.e., The intersection of the resulting Nyquist plot with the real axis), to be the result of the ionic resistances between one particle and its neighbors, instead of a lumped series resistance. A key consequence of this approach is a finite number of particles within electrode thickness, which leads to the existence of an optimal spatial distribution. For the reasons mentioned above, spatial structure is a fundamental aspect in battery modeling that has not been sufficiently explored in the literature [33], generally assuming a continuous geometry as a consequence of volume averaging [34], which may not necessarily match the actual spatial structure of the electrode and may constitute an important source of discrepancies between model and experimental results.

On the other hand, the analysis of voltage relaxation profiles after discharges at different C-rates remains a challenging problem [35], due to the wide range of amplitudes and characteristic time constants observed experimentally. This effect is often ascribed to electrode heterogeneity and modeled through the presence of a particle size distribution; nevertheless, it is unclear how to properly characterize it from experimental data and reproduce relaxation profiles for a wide range of C-rates. Kirk et al. [36] analyzed relaxation profiles for a Nickel Manganese Cobalt/Si-Gr (NMC/Si-Gr) cell after discharges at C/2, 1C and 1.5C, but did not consider a silicon phase and were unable to employ a unique particle size distribution for all three cases. In a recent work [35], Fernando et al. defined a comprehensive matrix of experiments for relaxation profiles at different SOCs, temperatures, and C-rates in commercial lithium ferrophosphate (LFP)/Gr and NMC/Gr cells, and fitted experimental data to first and second-order RC models with variable time constants. They demonstrated that relaxation profiles are heavily dependent on factors such as depth of discharge, temperature, current rate and chemistry; however, they did not identify clear correlations for the influence of temperature or current rate that could be useful to model or predict relaxation profiles. The recent work by Fath et al. [37] analyzes the effect of including a particle size distribution on NMC relaxation profiles via simulation, and verifies that a particle size distribution contributes to a slower voltage relaxation profile, but the effect of the current rate of the previous discharge on relaxation profiles is not considered. In summary, the analysis of voltage relaxation profiles remains an open research topic, as well as a relevant criterion for model validation.

Lastly, temperature constitutes a critical variable due to its major influence on battery behavior [38,39], and taking into account instantaneous cell temperature to update model parameters becomes essential to predict cell behavior when self-heating causes a considerable discrepancy between cell and ambient temperature, which is the case for operation at high C-rates and low temperatures [30]. Therefore, an accurate thermal model containing all components of heat generation, including the contribution of entropic heat and solid diffusion, is required to reproduce cell electrical and thermal response under a wider range of operating conditions [40]. Nevertheless, the computation of heat generation relies on the accuracy of the employed electrical model, which in turn also impacts cell temperature and model parameters as a result. Given the interrelation between thermal and electrical behavior, the development of high-fidelity electrothermal models, in combination with a suitable parameter set [41], is critical for accurately reproducing battery behavior and developing robust and optimized management systems.

For all of the reasons above, herein the concept of optimal spatial distribution is introduced and applied to the high-fidelity modeling of a commercial nickel-rich silicon-graphite battery cell. The procedure for incorporating a particle size distribution as well as the influence of cell temperature on model parameters within this reduced-order framework is also described. Simulation results show that the number of spatial nodes and the ohmic resistance are critical in reproducing discharge profiles at different current rates, while cell temperature and a particle size distribution are necessary to explain subsequent relaxation profiles with a unique set of parameters. Finally, the presented model is also validated experimentally for charges at C/2 and 1C, and the potential risk of lithium plating is discussed for the investigated cell, as indicated by the model, according to both the overpotential and surface saturation criteria.

The main contributions of this work are: (1) showing that the number of nodes in the spatial dimension of a DFN-type model is not an arbitrary value as the system is not necessarily spatially continuous, which leads to the existence of an optimal spatial distribution, (2) demonstrating the importance of considering instantaneous cell temperature and a particle size distribution in order to reproduce cell voltage and temperature for discharges at various C-rates, as well as the subsequent relaxation periods, with a single set of parameters, and (3) proving the predictive ability of the presented model by replicating charging behavior and anticipating potential fast charging limitations.

In a previous work [25], a novel ECM for cells with blended Si-Gr anodes was developed by taking the SPMe as a starting point, including a separate description for both components of the blend as well as an efficient method for the computation of the current distribution between them. The model is shown in Fig. 1 .

The previous model was able to provide accurate results in comparison to the DFN composite model in [21], although it required an adjustment of solid diffusion coefficients with current rate [42] upon validation against experimental data. This was shown to be an inherent limitation of single-particle approaches and a discrete rather than continuous spatial distribution along the electrode is able to reproduce complex experimental behavior as the current rate increases [30,40]. For this reason, the previous model in [25] is extended herein in order to incorporate an appropriate spatial distribution with the goal of better matching experimental data.

In order to define a spatial structure, the lumped ECM in Fig. 1 is expanded in a distributed manner as in Fig. 2 : at every spatial node in the cathode, there exists an NMC particle; whereas one silicon and one graphite particle at every spatial node in the anode. Each spatial node is connected to its neighbors via an ohmic resistance
R
o h m
, which symbolizes conduction losses in the electrolyte, as solid conductivity is usually orders of magnitude larger than electrolyte conductivity [27,43]. In this case,
R
o h m
also comprises the impedance due to concentration gradients in the electrolyte represented by
R e
and
C e
, as EIS data indicate that its magnitude is small compared to pure ohmic losses and its transient response is quick in relation to solid diffusion. This approach is similar to the transmission line model (TLM) applied to a porous electrode [43–45]; however, the main distinction lies in the consideration of a discrete spatial distribution by means of a macroscale ohmic resistance
R
o h m
and a finite number of particles, instead of the continuous spatial distribution of the standard TLM with a large number of particles N and resistance
R
o h m
/
N
[45]. The separator impedance has been neglected in this case due to shorter thickness and higher porosity [46].

A spatially discrete distribution is also more consistent with experimental EIS data, since a spatially continuous system does not lead to the observation of a macroscale ohmic resistance
R
o h m
in the high frequency limit, i.e., the intersection with the real axis, which has to be added as an external lumped element. Conversely, in the proposed approach,
R
o h m
is interpreted as the resistance for ionic transport between one particle and its neighbor, and is therefore integrated into the model structure. A key consequence of this hypothesis is that discretization is not arbitrary as the system is not spatially continuous, i.e., there exists an optimal number of points for both the positive electrode
N
P E
and the negative electrode
N
N E
, which may be interpreted as the average number of particles that fit sequentially within electrode thickness, and is able to provide the closest agreement with experimental data for across various C-rates.

A particle size distribution is incorporated according to the reduced-order procedure outlined in [30], which can be summarized as the parallel connection of several units composed of the Open Circuit Potential voltage source and the charge transfer resistance
R
c t
, as shown in Fig. 3 . One may note that, in this reduced-order modeling framework, the mathematical treatment of a particle size distribution is identical to that of a blended electrode.

3.
Calculation of charge transfer resistances and Thévenin equivalents
Once surface concentrations have been calculated for each particle, the exchange current

It is noted that the values of exchange current densities have to be calculated for each spatial node, as non-uniform particle charging and discharging may lead to different surface concentrations, therefore impacting individual and equivalent charge transfer resistances.
The exact expression for the charge transfer resistance in Eq. (2) presents a nonlinear dependency on the ratio between particle current I and exchange current
I 0
. For this reason, the consideration of this expression leads to a nonlinear system of equations for the current distribution in the electrode which must be solved numerically by iterative methods and is computationally expensive [43]. In order to circumvent this issue, herein the charge transfer resistances are updated as a function of the particle current in the previous timestep k − 1 , as shown in Eq. (6):

where Eq. (11b) accounts for the ionic current flowing through each ohmic resistance, which can be computed as the partial sums of electrode current up to the corresponding spatial node. For this reason, heat generation due to ohmic losses is anticipated to prevail over the other source terms, especially at higher currents.
The computed heat generation serves as the input to a lumped thermal model assuming a uniform cell temperature, which requires only two parameters: a thermal resistance
R

. Readers are kindly referred to previous works where this model is detailed and validated for cylindrical 18650-format cells [42,52–54] as thermal modeling is not the main focus of this work.

It is noted that, within the proposed approach, solid diffusion trails one timestep behind the current distribution, in the same way that parameters lag cell temperature. Nevertheless, the coupling among all processes is effective, provided that an adequate timestep Δ t is chosen in relation to the solid diffusion and thermal time constants.

In this article, a commercial Samsung-SDI INR18650-35 E cell was examined, which possesses a nominal discharge capacity ≥ 3350 mAh when discharged at C/2 within the voltage limits (4.2 V charge, 2.65 V discharge) according to the manufacturer, whereas the maximum continuous current limits stipulated by the manufacturer are 8 A (2.65C) for discharge and 2 A (0.6C) for charge. After conditioning procedures in accordance with United States Advanced Battery Consortium (USABC) guidelines [55] and thermodynamic cycling at C/50 and C/25, EIS experiments were conducted over a frequency range from 10 mHz to 10 kHz at various SOCs of 5 %, 20 %, 50 %, 80 %, and 95 %. Thereafter, galvanostatic discharges were conducted at C/2 (1.7 A), 1C (3.4 A), 1.5C (5 A) and 2C (6.8 A), all followed by a 2-h relaxation period, with the charging procedure being the standard constant-current-constant-voltage (CC-CV) at C/2 (1.7 A) to 4.2 V until C/50 (68 mA). Additionally, a charge process at 1C (i.e., beyond manufacturer limits) was performed in order to assess the fast-charging capability of this cell model. The experimental cycling tests were conducted using a multichannel, high-precision Arbin LBT battery cycler, while a Gamry Reference 3000 high-performance potentiostat/galvanostat was used for EIS testing. The cells were placed on a high-current cylindrical cell holder from Arbin, facilitating ultra-low impedance, 4-point connections. All experimental procedures were performed within a Memmert environmental chamber to ensure a constant ambient temperature of 23
∘
C. Readers are kindly referred to our previous work [25] for a more thorough description of experimental setup and methodology, which is only summarized herein to avoid redundancy.

In this section, the parameterization methodology for the presented ECM is described. First, the starting model hypotheses and available characterization data are specified.
1.
Quasi-static characterization. The first step in the parameterization process consists of full cell reconstruction from electrode characteristics. This was extensively discussed in our previous work [25], so it will only be summarized here for the sake of brevity. A mechanistic reconstruction was performed first for the negative electrode, yielding a 10 % silicon contribution to electrode capacity [56–58], and next for the full cell (see Fig. 4 ).
The results of the full-cell reconstruction are shown in Table 2 , along with the correlation between the SOC of the cell and the normalized lithium concentration in each material at SOC = 100 %, which are necessary for the initialization of the simulation. Analytical functions for OCPs are provided in Appendix A, which were previously employed in [57] and fitted in our previous work [25]; lithiation and delithiation branches are considered for both silicon and graphite.
Despite the close agreement between the experimental and reconstructed voltage profiles, the presence of silicon is understood to cause slight discrepancies in the low SOC range, specifically between 3 and 3.4 V, where its relative contribution to cell voltage is the greatest. This is due to the fact that the silicon-graphite blend is modeled in practice as the combination of the individual behaviors of each material [59] without considering potential additional interactions between them [7,60]. These deviations are also observed in several other works where full-cell reconstruction for cells containing Si-Gr blended electrodes is performed based on individual OCP profiles [21,57,58].
2.
EIS data. The experimentally measured frequency responses at 5 %, 20 %, 50 %, 80 %, and 95 % SOC at 23
∘
C are shown in Fig. 5 . In this article, EIS data will be employed to establish a range for the overall ohmic resistance

3.
Arrhenius activation energies. Parameters

4.
Parameter set from Chen et al. [ 46 ]. Chen et al. [46] performed extensive electrochemical characterization tests on both electrodes for a commercial Ni-rich, Si-Gr cell, and provided a parameter set for simulations within the DFN framework. Parameters related to the solid diffusion and charge transfer processes for all three materials have been taken as a starting point for further adjustments.

The optimization process is described by the following sequence of steps, each one lumping together parameters with a similar observed degree of sensitivity. An advantage of the ECM formulation with respect to a standard DFN lies in the fact that not all the electrochemical parameters need to be determined individually, just their groupings as resistors or time constants.
1.
Determination of the number of spatial nodes in each electrode.
As detailed in Section 2, the physically motivated transmission line model yields qualitatively different results depending on whether the spatial distribution of particles in each electrode is considered to be continuous or discrete, characterized by a transport resistance
R
o h m
or
R

/

Although a continuous spatial distribution is a consequence of volume averaging, we showed that a discrete spatial distribution is more consistent with experimental EIS results [30,40], as well as cross-sectional images of experimentally harvested electrodes [46]. However, if a discrete spatial distribution is considered, the number of spatial nodes in each electrode
N
P E
and

become essential model parameters and a set of suitable criteria is required to determine them. The values of average particle radius and electrode thickness were measured experimentally by Chen et al. [46] for a commercial NMC/Si-Gr cell, which allows for an initial estimation of the number of spatial nodes (

However, in the case of materials with a flat or stepwise flat OCP, such as graphite, a growing number of points produces a voltage vs. time curve with an increasingly steeper slope for a constant ohmic resistance. Following these considerations, the operation of the model is simulated with the initial parameters described previously at two different discharge rates (1.5C and 2C) and compared to the corresponding experimental results. The slope of the voltage response is the main criteria for identifying parameter

Regarding the particle size distribution (PSD), previous works have determined it experimentally [46] and fitted it to an analytical probability distribution; however, other authors [36] have pointed out that the PSD measured experimentally does not necessarily provide the closest agreement with full-cell experimental data. For this reason, in this paper the PSD has been determined indirectly from experimental full-cell relaxation profiles instead of directly from destructive particle size measurements. To this end, three representative particle sizes

have been considered to cover the full range of the PSD reported in [36]. Next, the capacity associated with each particle size has been adjusted to match relaxation profiles; it is verified that the fraction of capacity associated with the largest particle size has the greatest impact on relaxation profiles. The reason for including such a large particle size is due to the fact that outlier particles, despite not contributing to electrode capacity to a great extent, may dictate the relaxation process [37]. Furthermore, particles are assumed to be spherical in shape and homogeneously distributed, while in reality they may exhibit irregular shapes that result in a slower lithium redistribution [63]. Conversely, parameters
R

τ

R

is adjusted by refining the slope of the voltage response and the voltage level towards the end of the discharges.

Identified model parameters are summarized in Table 3 .

In this section, the proposed model with the parameters specified in the previous section is validated against experimental voltage profiles for discharges at C/2 (1.7 A), 1C (3.4 A), 1.5C (5 A) and 2C (6.8 A), each followed by a 2-h relaxation period. It is noted that only the most relevant internal states will be shown in the main text; the evolution of all internal states for each process is provided in the Supplementary data to avoid cluttering.

A general outlook of simulation and experimental results is provided in Fig. 7 , with Root Mean Square (RMS) error results summarized in Table 4 . It is noted that only the delithiation branches of silicon and graphite OCPs are employed for the complete process, since the hysteresis state is assumed not to change when current is interrupted instead of reversed. It is observed that output voltage is closely matched for all current rates: the progressive linearization of the OCP characteristics is reproduced, and the increasing slope and decreasing level of voltage profiles vs. time are captured. This is a distinctive feature of the discretely distributed topology, enabled by the determination of the optimal value of the number of points N and the ohmic resistance
R
o h m
in each electrode as described in the previous section. It is also pointed out that equilibrium voltage is accurately predicted and relaxation profiles are qualitatively replicated with the same set of parameter values for the four considered C-rates, which is ascribed to the consideration of the effect of instantaneous cell temperature as well as a particle size distribution.

Next, a precise description of internal model behavior is depicted in Fig. 8 for the case of a 1.5C discharge and the subsequent 2-h relaxation period. With respect to the positive electrode, given its quasi-linear OCP, the current distribution is mainly uniform except for the initial part. However, some fluctuations are observed due to the presence of discretely distributed ohmic resistance
R
o h m
, which causes the particles located closer to the separator to reach an increasingly higher degree of lithiation. This results in a potential gradient in the electrode that, once current is interrupted, forces a quick redistribution of lithium along the electrode: NMC particles 1 and 2 (next to the separator) delithiate, while particles 3 through 6 (adjacent to the current collector) continue lithiating, and the equilibrium concentration is reached sooner than in the case where a spatial distribution is not considered. As for the negative electrode, the stepwise flat graphite OCP results in an almost completely sequential delithiation for each particle size: the smallest graphite particle adjacent to the separator reaches below 10 % SOC before its counterparts next to the current collector reach 70 % SOC. This is also corroborated by the current distribution, where sequential discharge peaks are clearly observed and indicate the degree of utilization of each particle throughout the discharge. Although silicon utilization is concentrated towards the final segment of the discharge, the discrete spatial distribution causes silicon particle 1 (adjacent to the separator) to delithiate in a sustained manner, while particles 5 and 6 (adjacent to the current collector) remain largely inactive until the end. Regarding relaxation in the negative electrode, higher current rates induce a greater degree of utilization of smaller particles (silicon and the smallest graphite particle size in this case) with respect to their relative capacity contribution due to their lower impedance, as well as a large potential gradient along the electrode. As a result, relaxation after a discharge at a high rate appears to occur in two phases: first, a quick lithium redistribution along the electrode at a higher temperature dictated by small particles, which forces a quick potential recovery close to the equilibrium potential. Thereafter, a slow redistribution follows between different particle sizes at the same node, whose limiting process is solid diffusion in large particles. However, due to the large initial potential recovery, the second redistribution stage does not have a significant impact on voltage. Conversely, after a C/2 discharge, cell temperature rise and the potential gradient in the x dimension are negligible; therefore, the relaxation process is dictated by an unforced relaxation of larger particles, as shown in Fig. 1 in Supplementary data.

The surface concentration of silicon particles as a function of their spatial position is shown in Fig. 9 for the investigated C-rates. It is noted that silicon utilization is heavily dependent on current rate and spatial position. At C/2, the potential drop in the ohmic resistance is reduced, which causes all particles to delithiate approximately simultaneously, with their operation being concentrated towards the end of the discharge. As the current rate increases, the concentration distribution along the spatial dimension becomes progressively more spread out, with particles situated closest to the separator starting to delithiate sooner, and particles closest to the current collector remaining largely inactive until late stages of the process, at which point they are quickly discharged. In the extreme case of a 2C discharge, particle 1 delithiates in a sustained manner throughout the process, while particle 6 only delithiates halfway. This non-uniform delithiation as a consequence of the distributed macroscale ohmic resistance may result in heterogeneous degradation as a function of spatial position depending on the operating regime. This is due to the fact that well-connected particles provide a greater discharge capacity on average, whereas poorly-connected particles experience both inactivity periods and large current peaks, which may be crucial in the computation of loss of active material.

Additionally, the presented model with the same parameter values is also validated for constant-current charges at C/2 and 1C; in this case, the lithiation branches of the silicon and graphite OCPs are employed. Experimental and simulation results are depicted in Fig. 10 a) and b); it is noted that charge profiles are also accurately reproduced with respective RMS voltage errors of 12.7 and 10.8 mV, respectively. Similarly to discharge, at C/2 silicon utilization is restricted to low SOCs, whereas at 1C it is marginally spread out due to the spatial distribution (see Fig. 10c) and d)). With respect to graphite, the surface concentration of the smallest particle size as a function of spatial position is depicted in Fig. 10e) and f). It is observed that graphite lithiation is slightly more simultaneous compared to delithiation due to a less abrupt shape of its lithiation OCP; however, the particle located closest to the separator (particle 1) still reaches a full degree of lithiation by the end of the discharge, while particles 5 and 6 only reach 0.6 and 0.5 at C/2 and 1C, respectively. As a result, the most relevant information about internal states for the onset of lithium plating is related to the surface concentration of the silicon and graphite particles situated closest to the separator, following the surface saturation criterion [64,65], along with the negative electrode potential ϕ =
ϕ s
−
ϕ e
, shown in Fig. 10e) and f), according to the overpotential criterion [64,66]. It is noted that towards the end of the C/2 charge,
ϕ 1
reaches a minimum value of 23 mV, while the maximum surface concentrations for graphite and silicon approach and marginally exceed 1, respectively. In contrast, during a 1C charge (above manufacturer limits),
ϕ 1
crosses the 0 V threshold at 0.64 h, approximately at the same time when graphite particle 1 surpasses the maximum concentration, indicating a high risk of lithium plating for the particle located closest to the separator. Interestingly, silicon particle 1 reaches a surface concentration greater than 1 at 0.55 h, which suggests that the presence of silicon may lead to an earlier onset of lithium plating [67,68], and therefore impose stricter constraints on the charging current limits. We hypothesize that this is one of the main reasons for the maximum charging current limit set by the manufacturer to 2 A (0.6C), which may also be a relevant cause for the poor fast-charging performance of these cells reported in previous literature [26,69], in addition to silicon particle swelling that has not been considered in this work. Nevertheless, the validation of this hypothesis constitutes the basis of future work, given the challenge posed by the determination of absolute stoichiometry limits. Additional simulation results that illustrate this effect at different C-rates and temperatures are included in Fig. S8 in Supplementary data for reference.

Lastly, the progression of cell temperature for the processes considered is summarized in Fig. 11 , where the temperature increase with respect to ambient temperature Δ T is depicted along with the simulated total heat generation. Details about the calibration of the thermal model may be found in our previous work [40], and error results are summarized in Table 4. It is noted that highly accurate results are obtained as the qualitative thermal response is captured due to the consideration of all heat generation mechanisms. An interesting comparison may be made with regard to the difference in thermal behavior in charge vs. discharge, related to the implications of the spatial distribution on heat generation.

During charge, as stated above, the current distribution is more homogeneous, especially in the anode, due to a less abrupt graphite OCP. In consequence, the corresponding heat generation is approximately constant throughout the process, which causes cell temperature to stabilize around a certain value. In contrast, the current distribution in the anode is increasingly sequential with higher current rates. A key consequence of this is that current has to travel through a growing number of ohmic resistances to reach the poorly connected particles as the discharge advances. As a result, Joule heating increases progressively and reaches a much higher value by the end of the discharge, which causes the temperature profile to continue growing linearly throughout the discharges. This behavior is supported by the fact that ohmic losses are the main contributors to heat generation in all cases, and in particular anode ohmic losses at higher rates, as depicted in Fig. 7 in Supplementary data.

With the goal of illustrating the importance of an appropriate spatial structure, different modeling alternatives are compared to experimental discharges at 1C and 1.5C, followed by their respective relaxation periods. The considered cases are spatially lumped or distributed (Figs. 1 and 2) with and without a particle size distribution (PSD), and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 12 with the same parameter values.

Two distinct behaviors are observed: during the constant-current phase, the spatial distribution becomes essential to match the voltage as well as its increasing slope, whereas this qualitative behavior cannot be replicated by a spatially lumped model with a single set of parameters. Although adjusting parameters as a function of the current rate can help to achieve a closer response [25,42], the progressive smoothing and increasing slope of the voltage response are distinct features of the spatially distributed model [30,40]. In contrast, the presence of a particle size distribution marginally affects the voltage response at the end of the discharge, but it has a major impact on relaxation profiles. It is observed that, without a particle size distribution, both the spatially lumped and distributed models produce a relaxation response that is faster than the experimental results. However, both the spatially lumped and distributed models with a particle size distribution are able to achieve a close match for both current rates. Therefore, it is concluded that an appropriate spatial distribution is crucial to replicate the voltage and its slope during constant-current operation, whereas a particle size distribution is required to reproduce voltage relaxation profiles, and both elements can be combined within the framework presented in this work.

In order to highlight the importance of considering the effect of self-heating on model parameters, model operation is also simulated assuming a constant cell temperature for 1.5C and 2C discharges, as shown in Fig. 13 . In these cases, cell temperature rises 11
∘
C and 24
∘
C over ambient temperature, respectively. In consequence, maintaining parameters constant at their value at 23
∘
C overestimates cell impedance and results in an excessive decrease in cell voltage, causing it to hit the lower voltage cutoff earlier than observed experimentally. This is also a major factor in explaining relaxation profiles, as the initial phase of relaxation occurs at the highest temperature, which aids in achieving a faster response that cannot be obtained if solid diffusion time constants are not updated with cell temperature.

Lastly, the limitations of the presented approach are discussed, and further extensions of this work are outlined. First, we have made several modeling assumptions that, despite being reasonably substantiated, may be refined to slightly improve the match between simulation and experimental results. For instance, Arrhenius activation energies
E a
have been fixed beforehand and are equal for all materials for each process as representative values, which is not necessarily true but avoids artificially increasing the number of free parameters to be determined and thus, prevents overfitting. We acknowledge that employing individual activation energies for each material may provide a closer agreement with experimental data; however, identifying clear thermal dependencies for each process in each material becomes increasingly challenging, especially from full-cell data. The vector of relative particle sizes has also been preset, as we have observed that not doing so hinders the convergence of the optimization process and does not substantially modify simulation results. A finer distribution of a greater number of particle sizes is likely to provide a smoother voltage response during the initial phase of relaxation, at the expense of an increase in computational cost and a more difficult determination of their associated capacities nonetheless. The charge transfer resistance corresponding to NMC
R
c t , N M C
has been assumed to be negligible according to separate experimental electrode measurements in [46]; we have also verified that leaving it as a fitting parameter does not noticeably improve simulation results. In summary, we leave open the possibility of further refinement of the parameter set presented in this article.

Future research also includes experimental validation of the model during relaxation periods at different SOCs and temperatures in order to extend its range of applicability, as well as analysis of the influence of spatial asymmetry between both electrodes on the experimentally measured full cell frequency response. Additionally, a direct extension of this work is related to the analysis of the appropriate spatial distribution for an electrode of a high-power cell with respect to that of a high-energy cell (like the one considered in this work), due to design choices regarding electrode thickness, particle size and porosity [70], which result in a potentially different electrode spatial structure.

An aspect that has not been addressed in this article is related to model validation under dynamic operation, with frequent switches in current sign. Given the magnitude of hysteresis observed in silicon (and to a lesser extent, in graphite), as well as the possibly different underlying mechanisms, the consideration and validation of dynamic hysteresis in a multi-particle system are beyond the scope of this work. In any case, the presented model can yield a close agreement with experimental galvanostatic operation both in charge and discharge at different C-rates, as well as during relaxation periods after discharges, while also offering insightful information about internal battery states and incorporating effects such as a particle size distribution and self-heating on model parameters. Despite the apparent model complexity, the ECM formulation along with the presented simulation framework makes the proposed approach computationally efficient. In order to illustrate this, the simulation times associated with each process are detailed in Table 5 ; it is noted that, although a constant timestep Δ t is employed throughout each process, it may change as a function of the C-rate since higher currents require finer sampling to preserve numerical stability.

The employed timestep Δ t is constrained by numerical stability in the regions where the slopes of Open Circuit Potentials are large. In this case, this corresponds to high SOCs due to NMC, and low SOCs due to graphite. Therefore, potentially superior performance could be achieved if sophisticated adaptive time-stepping algorithms were implemented, yielding a finer grid in these regions and a coarser sampling elsewhere. In any case, although there is room for improvement in the numerical implementation in this work, we understand that the proposed modeling approach enables a potentially higher degree of computational efficiency in comparison to a full-order coupled thermal-electrochemical model, while maintaining accuracy.

Finally, as an example of an additional application of the presented model, a multi-step charging protocol is designed according to the overpotential criterion for lithium plating, such that the current is decreased as soon as electrode potential
ϕ s
−
ϕ e
for the first spatial node reaches 0 V. As shown in Fig. 14 , the initial charging current is set to 1.5C, and subsequently decreased to 1C, 0.75C and finally to 0.5C.

Two interesting observations may be made in this regard: first, reaching the upper cutoff voltage at any rate above 0.5C (the standard charging current set by the manufacturer) results in a potential below 0 V, which we believe is an indication of the reliability of the presented model. Second, the duration of the CC charging phase is reduced from 1.5 h at the standard rate of 0.5C (see Fig. 10) to about 45 min, without allegedly inducing additional degradation due to lithium plating, which is a 50 % decrease in charging time. The design of this protocol would not be possible without relevant information about internal states, which is supported by the accuracy in the experimental validation of different scenarios with a single set of parameters. In consequence, the experimental validation of multi-step charging protocols constitutes the basis of further research.

In this article, a reduced-order modeling framework is developed for the high-fidelity modeling of galvanostatic operation and relaxation profiles for a commercial nickel-rich, silicon-graphite cell. The proposed model includes a separate treatment of the silicon and graphite phases, a particle size distribution for graphite, and the influence of self-heating on model parameters. A novel contribution of this work is the concept of optimal spatial distribution, i.e., the consideration of the number of spatial nodes in each electrode as a key model parameter to explain cell voltage at increasing C-rates. Furthermore, a particle size distribution as well as the consideration of instantaneous temperature is shown to be necessary to reproduce relaxation profiles.

The previous considerations allow the model to provide accurate results when validated against experimental data: ≤ 20 mV RMS for constant current discharges from C/2 up to 2C, ≤ 15 mV RMS for discharges followed by relaxation, ≤ 13 mV RMS for charges at C/2 and 1C, and ≤ 0.35
∘
C RMS for cell temperature in all cases with a single set of parameters. The proposed model provides valuable information about internal cell states in order to accurately quantify silicon utilization as a function of the current rate, and to anticipate the fast-charging limit for the onset of lithium plating. Future research entails the appropriate description of spatial structure for other cell chemistries and formats. In conclusion, we understand that the concept of optimal spatial distribution introduced herein constitutes a relevant factor in the development of high-fidelity battery models.
